`session.mount()` works when passed `mock` but not when `https` - for https to work, it needs to be passed `https://`. I'm not sure why this is, but the [documentation](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/user/advanced/#transport-adapters) suggests this approach fits fine. It required a few changes elsewhere, removing `://` from a few string constructors. The alternative to doing that is to inject a `://` into the `session.mount()` call, but I chose not to do that in case, in theory, you wanted to use a longer prefix like `http://google.com` (which would work with this approach)

i.e. we could do:
```python
import six
...snip...

with open(file_path, mode='wb') as f:
    if six.PY3:
        report = report.encode('utf-8')
    f.write(report)
```

It's a bit ugly, but basically the string in Python 2.7 is a byte string, and the default system encoding is ascii, which is why it breaks with just mode `w` as it tries to encode a `utf-8` string ascii. Changing the mode to `wb` fixes this as then it doesn't try to encode it further when writing. But, in Python 3, the string is unicode so needs to be encoded as bytes before being written with mode `b` (but would work fine if the mode was just `w`).

This is puzzling. I'm not sure what the original was intended to do -- the `str()` bit in `str(new_data.encode('utf-8'))` is redundant on Python 2.7. Seems like `new_data` is just supposed to be a bytes object, so can't we just do `new_data.encode('utf-8')` instead?

True - it's a bit odd, because the only reason to specify MIDI velocities is so that this line makes sense:
> `velocity_range = max(1, max_velocity - min_velocity)`
It's ok if `max_velocity - min_velocity = 0` because that just means all the velocities in the reference annotation are the same velocity, but if `0 < max_velocity - min_velocity < 1` then it's a bit odd to clip it to 1.  Instead of picking some epsilon, I thought it would be simpler to just specify velocity should be MIDI velocity.  It actually doesn't matter if the velocity range is [0, 127] or [0, N] for some large N, since the velocity range will just get rescaled to `[0, 1]` anyways.  So there's not really any reason to check for `< 127`, if that makes sense.

`bravado_config` is a namedtuple object, calling set on it will return the content of the different fields
```python
>>> C = namedtuple('C', 'a, b, c')
>>> instance = C(1,2,3)
>>> print instance
C(a=1, b=2, c=3)
>>> set(instance)
set([1, 2, 3])
>>> set(C._fields)
set(['a', 'c', 'b'])
>>> 
```

It's necessary because of the way we do testing, and the way we do packaging:

* Our wheels (when packaged) don't include tests, for size/brevity.
* Our tests expect to run in-package and do relative/local imports, which fail if the tests are outside the package.

It probably would be simpler to just include the tests in the packaged wheel. I doubt it saves much in terms of size.

The code is certainly improved from last time. However, the central algorithm can still be simplified, basically by merging the checks for `AugAssign` and `Name` nodes. Restructure your code as follows:

1. Obtain a list of *all* `AssignName` and `Name` nodes that have the same name as the loop target, and whose `.lookup` method call returns that target. Note that `nodes_of_class` can take in multiple node classes.
2. Check whether *all* of the nodes from the previous step are "redundant". This can be done using just a single helper function that takes a `AssignName` or `Name` node and determines whether it's "redundant"; also, this flips the boolean output from what you had before, so be careful here.
3. Return the correct thing. Remember to check for whether there's at least one usage of the loop target at all.

Note that this strategy will reduce the overall number of helper functions.
